source.title: Short Average Duration of NREM/REM Cycle Is Related to Cognitive Decline in an Elderly Cohort: An Exploratory Investigation.

confidence_rationale: Prospective community cohort (n=235) age 60+, baseline PSG, 4-year MCI/dementia conversion outcome (n=14 incident cases). Short average NREM/REM cycle duration AND short average REM duration per cycle each independently predicted incident MCI/dementia. The cycle-level decomposition is exactly the kind of architecture-specific signal Q-032's PD-biomarker proposal needs -- it dissociates phase-specific metrics from total sleep time. But the small case count (n=14) caps confidence. Author labels this exploratory.
failure_signatures: n=14 incident cases is small. The OR estimates have wide confidence intervals and replication in larger cohorts is not yet available.
Single baseline PSG, not within-person change. Cannot distinguish people whose REM was always short from people whose REM declined recently.
Authors describe the analysis as exploratory; no Bonferroni correction across multiple architecture metrics tested. Type-I risk is non-trivial.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In a community-dwelling Korean cohort age 60+ with baseline polysomnography (n=235), short average NREM/REM cycle duration (OR 0.97 per minute, 95% CI 0.94-0.99, p=0.02) and short average REM sleep duration per cycle (OR 0.87 per minute, 95% CI 0.76-0.98, p=0.03) were each significantly associated with conversion to MCI or dementia at 4-year follow-up (14/235 converters). Authors frame this as exploratory evidence that NREM/REM cycle architecture predicts cognitive decline beyond conventional individual-stage metrics.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-032 limb (a): supports the architecture-specific PD-biomarker proposal at the cycle level. The cycle-level decomposition is methodologically interesting because it captures temporal architecture (the alternation between NREM and REM phases, MECH-120 through MECH-123 in the REE pipeline-phase model) rather than just stage-aggregate percentages. This matches the REE framing that pipeline integrity depends on the COORDINATION of phases, not just total time spent in each phase. The fact that REM-per-cycle adds predictive value over total REM% is exactly the cycle-coordination signal an architecture-aware Q-032 biomarker would capture.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Small case count (n=14) and exploratory framing limit the strength of the inference. The signal could be a chance finding from multiple-comparison testing across architecture metrics. Replication in larger cohorts (e.g. SHHS, MrOS, Framingham) is not yet available. Single baseline PSG cannot distinguish always-short-REM from recent-REM-decline.
mapping.source_context: Korean Longitudinal Study on Cognitive Aging and Dementia (KLOSCAD), n=235 community-dwelling cognitively normal elderly age 60+, baseline overnight PSG, 4-year follow-up for MCI/dementia conversion (n=14, 5.9%), logistic regression analyses.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality moderate: J Alzheimers Dis is reputable but exploratory; small n=14 cases dominates uncertainty. Mapping fidelity good because cycle-level decomposition matches the REE pipeline-coordination framing. Transfer risk moderate-to-high because (i) replication needed and (ii) Korean elderly may differ from broader populations in cycle architecture norms.
